summarization.a: shows TDMAT strongly chemisorbing onto the clean Ru(0001) surface. Both side and top views highlight Ti–surface interaction, with a strong adsorption energy of −2.23 eV, indicating high precursor affinity for Ru.
summarization.b: demonstrates that once Ru(0001) is pre-covered with aniline, TDMAT adsorption weakens substantially (−0.87 eV). The views illustrate steric blocking and reduced contact area with the surface.
summarization.c: displays TDMAT adsorption on clean Co(0001), showing moderate binding (−1.86 eV). The geometry suggests less surface–precursor interaction compared to Ru but still strong enough to support growth.
summarization.d: shows TDMAT adsorption on aniline-functionalized Co(0001), with greatly reduced adsorption (−0.90 eV). Aniline blocks active sites similarly to Ru, reducing precursor adhesion.

d.question: How does weakened TDMAT adsorption on aniline-functionalized surfaces support area-selective ALD?
d.answer: When aniline pre-adsorbs on Ru or Co, it blocks active sites that TDMAT requires for chemisorption, reducing ΔEₐds by more than half. This suppression of strong binding prevents precursor attachment, effectively inhibiting growth on those metal regions. Meanwhile, growth can still proceed on unfunctionalized areas such as oxides, enabling controlled area-selective ALD through chemical contrast in adsorption strength.

b.question: Which condition shows the strongest adsorption of TDMAT among the four panels?
b.answer: TDMAT adsorbs most strongly on bare Ru(0001) with ΔEₐds = −2.23 eV.

a.question: Does functionalizing Ru(0001) or Co(0001) with aniline change the workflow for computing TDMAT adsorption energies?
a.answer: Yes — the DFT workflow must include an initial optimization of the aniline-covered surface before placing the TDMAT precursor.

c.question: Which structural factors influence the adsorption strength of TDMAT on the metal surfaces?
c.answer: Presence or absence of aniline functionalization, Surface metal type (Ru vs Co), Accessibility of metal active sites, Orientation of the TDMAT ligand cage, Degree of steric blocking by adsorbed aniline
